title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, July 14
tldr: Web-dev play of the day: bots are now the majority of web traffic and the new ones behave like people over a whole session — so turn on session-based bot detection, not a one-time CAPTCHA. Cloudflare shipped Precursor (Jul 13): instead of a per-request challenge a bot beats by refreshing the page, it drops in a little JavaScript that watches the whole visit — mouse-movement arcs, keystroke rhythm (the timing, NOT the keys you press), focus and tab-visibility — and scores whether the session is a human or an automated/AI agent in real time. An agent can't reset that signature by reloading. Cloudflare pegs bots at ~57% of all web requests (first time bots outnumber humans). Run it today: if a site you ship is on Cloudflare Bot Management, switch Precursor on for the zone in observation mode first (log, don't block), read the human-vs-agent split for a week, then challenge unverified sessions on your sensitive routes (login, checkout, signup, API). No app changes — the script auto-injects; free to use until GA later this year. Not on Bot Management (Enterprise)? The free, any-plan equivalent for your highest-risk forms is Turnstile. Honest scope: it's JavaScript-based (blind to no-JS clients) and pre-GA. Card: card-webdev-cloudflare-precursor.

Growth play: the ground under top-of-funnel content just moved — new causal data shows AI chat is measurably shrinking informational search, and the answer engine keeps the click — so start measuring your AI-answer presence, not just your Google rank. A Bocconi University study (arXiv, Jul 8; Comscore panel of 45,000+ US households) found that widening ChatGPT access cut weekly traditional-search queries 9.4% right away, growing to 17% after 20 weeks. The damage is concentrated exactly where how-to/reference content lives: academic −32.8%, reference/knowledge −26.5%, developer/technical −15.1%, news −13.4%; transactional and entertainment searches barely moved. And ChatGPT sends a visitor out to a website in only 5.2% of sessions vs Google's 31.1% — so even a win inside the answer yields far fewer clicks. You can't out-blog this; you win by being the cited source in the answer and by tracking whether you're named at all. Run it today with HubSpot's 4-step method + free tools: baseline in Google Search Console → Generative AI, run the free HubSpot AEO Grader (+ Semrush's free checker / Bing AI Citation Share) across ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity in logged-out sessions, track mentions vs owned citations vs share-of-voice, then run the AEO formatting pass on the money pages you're absent from. (Ranking #1 ≠ getting cited: Semrush found the top organic result is used as a citation only ~34% of the time on mobile / 46% on desktop.) Card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated).

Email play: your biggest-revenue sends of the year land in the window where the inbox is most fragile — so do your holiday deliverability prep NOW (mid-July), not in October. The mistake teams make is ramping volume the week they need it: providers read a sudden Q4 volume spike as a spam signal and junk you at the worst possible moment. Litmus's summer prep guide (Jul 9) is the calendar leg of deliverability — reputation is built over weeks, so September should be a gradual ramp, not an October cliff. Run it today: (1) auth audit — SPF/DKIM/DMARC across every sending domain + IP, DMARC at least p=quarantine (ideally p=reject), and enroll Google Postmaster + Microsoft SNDS/JMRP + Yahoo Sender Hub; (2) clean the list now — run a validation tool to drop invalids and segment by clicks/purchases/site behaviour, not opens (Apple's proxy auto-opens lie), remembering Yahoo purges inactive accounts after ~12 months and Gmail after ~24; (3) re-engage-then-suppress the disengaged; (4) set a valid reply-to (Microsoft requires it) and stand up a preference center for zero-party data; (5) start the volume ramp in September, in small increments — never a sudden spike. Card: card-email-holiday-deliverability-prep.

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; same-day market prints are directional): Apple sued OpenAI for trade-secret theft, and a macro (oil + rates) shock — not an AI-thesis break — knocked the chip trade Monday. Apple sued OpenAI in federal court (N.D. Cal., Jul 10) alleging OpenAI stole trade secrets 'at every level' to build consumer hardware: it says OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan (an ex-Apple VP) had Apple job candidates bring 'actual parts' to interviews for 'show and tell,' that an ex-engineer kept an Apple laptop and downloaded confidential docs, and that OpenAI coached departing staff to evade exit security (OpenAI: 'We have no interest in other companies' trade secrets'). It's the sharp turn from their 2024 partnership, chilled since OpenAI bought Jony Ive's io Products for ~$6.4B to enter hardware. Separately, Monday Jul 13 saw an AI-chip selloff (Nvidia ~−3.5%, Micron ~−4.3%, AMD ~−4.2%, Marvell ~−7.8%, SanDisk ~−12.6%; Nasdaq ~−1.55%) driven by a US–Iran / Strait-of-Hormuz oil spike + inflation-rebound and Fed-rate-hike worries — a macro shock, not a capex-confidence break. It pressures the open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20], which we hold open and judge at the window on closes. No news card (landscape = intake).

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is the web is being reshaped by machines reading and acting for humans — so meet each shift where it actually happens. Bots crossed 50% of traffic and learned to fake a human session, so detection has to watch the whole journey, not one request (Precursor). AI chat is quietly draining your informational-search traffic and keeping the click, so the scoreboard moves from 'do I rank?' to 'am I the cited answer?' — and you can only fix what you measure (AEO presence tracking). Your peak-season revenue rides on an inbox that grades you over weeks, so the warm-up starts in July, not October. And even a courtroom fight over 'actual parts' in an interview is really the same story — the AI-hardware land grab — reminding you that provenance and IP are now procurement questions on the work we ship. Read the shift, then act at its layer.

sections.h: Web dev — run it today: bots are now most of your traffic and the new ones fake a whole human session, so turn on Cloudflare Precursor (session-based agent detection), not a one-shot CAPTCHA

blocks.sub: Cloudflare Precursor (shipped Jul 13 2026) injects lightweight JavaScript that continuously scores a visitor across the WHOLE session — mouse-movement arcs, keystroke rhythm (timing, not the actual keys), focus + tab-visibility — to tell a human from an automated/AI agent in real time. Unlike a per-request CAPTCHA that a bot beats by refreshing, an agent can't reset its behavioural signature by reloading the page. Cloudflare puts bots at ~57% of all web requests (first time bots outnumber humans). It's part of Bot Management, free to enable until GA later in 2026; no app changes (script auto-injected). Not on Bot Management? Turnstile (free, any plan) is the run-it-today equivalent for your highest-risk forms.
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: for the first time, automated traffic has eclipsed human traffic — Cloudflare puts bots at roughly 57% of all web requests — and the hard part is that modern bots and AI browsing agents no longer look obviously robotic: they render JavaScript, move a cursor, and behave plausibly across a full visit. The old defence — a challenge on a single request (a CAPTCHA, a Turnstile widget, a per-request bot score) — is a snapshot: pass it once, or just refresh, and the agent is through. On Jul 13 2026 Cloudflare shipped Precursor, which flips the model from snapshot to continuous, session-based verification. It dynamically injects a small script into your HTML responses that collects behavioural signals across the whole user journey — the physical fingerprints of a real person that bots struggle to fake consistently: the arc of a wrist pivoting a mouse, the cognitive-load pauses and rhythm of typing, focus changes, tab visibility. Crucially it captures keystroke timing and rhythm, not the actual keys pressed, and analyses signals as aggregate patterns, not tied to a user's identity (Cloudflare's privacy framing). Those signals feed its bot score in real time, and — because the score is built over the session, not per request — an automated agent can't reset its signature by reloading a page or hitting a fresh URL. Why it matters for us: the sites we ship increasingly bleed from bot behaviour that a one-time gate misses — skewed analytics and ad metrics, content scraping to train competitors, credential-stuffing on login, fake-signup and form spam, and AI agents transacting on flows never designed for them. Precursor is the frontend/edge lever that raises the cost of faking a human without dropping a friction wall in every real visitor's face. Honest scope: it's a JavaScript-based signal, so it's blind to no-JS clients and shouldn't be your only control (keep server-side rate limits + auth checks); it's part of Cloudflare Bot Management (Enterprise tier) and pre-GA — Cloudflare says it's free to use until general availability later this year, so treat it as an early-access capability to pilot, not a set-and-forget guarantee; and it complements, doesn't replace, Turnstile.
blocks.doIt: (1) If a site you run is on Cloudflare Bot Management: turn Precursor on for the zone from the dashboard in a low-friction observation mode first — it scores sessions and logs, but doesn't block. No code changes; Cloudflare auto-injects the script. (2) Read the split for ~a week: look at how much of your traffic verifies as human vs unverified/agent on your key routes before you enforce anything — you're calibrating, not guessing. (3) Then enforce on sensitive routes only: require a challenge for unverified sessions on login, checkout, signup, password-reset and public API/GraphQL endpoints — the places where a fake session actually costs money — and leave content pages low-friction. (4) Keep your other layers: pair it with server-side rate limiting, one-click List-Unsubscribe/auth on forms, and standard WAF rules — Precursor is a behavioural signal, not a firewall. (5) Not on Enterprise Bot Management? Ship Turnstile (free, works on any Cloudflare plan) on your highest-risk forms today as the privacy-first CAPTCHA replacement, and put Precursor on the roadmap for when you need session-continuous detection. (6) Sanity-check privacy/consent: confirm with your team that behavioural-signal collection fits your cookie/consent posture before enabling in the EU. Card: card-webdev-cloudflare-precursor.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (≥3) — Cloudflare's own 'Introducing Precursor' engineering blog (primary: continuous session-based signals, injected JS, keystroke-timing-not-keys, aggregate-not-identity, ~57% bots, free until GA later this year, complements Turnstile, part of Bot Management) plus SiliconANGLE and Help Net Security (independent write-ups; SiliconANGLE frames it 'generally available now' while Cloudflare's blog says free-until-GA — we publish the more conservative pre-GA framing) and a BusinessWire release. Honest scope: JavaScript-based (blind to no-JS clients); Enterprise Bot Management tier; pricing not disclosed; the 57% bot-share figure is Cloudflare's own. Seeds thread-agentic-web-defense (the browser/edge is now an agent-vs-human boundary) — sibling to the AI-toolchain-attack-surface + least-privilege threads (card-webdev-rotate-ai-toolchain, card-webdev-cloudflare-temporary-accounts). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major edge/bot vendor ships continuous session-based agent detection). Sources: Cloudflare Blog, SiliconANGLE, Help Net Security, BusinessWire.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: new causal data shows AI chat is measurably shrinking informational search AND keeping the click, so move your scoreboard from 'do I rank?' to 'am I the cited answer?' — and measure it

blocks.sub: A Bocconi University study (arXiv, Jul 8 2026; Comscore desktop panel of 45,000+ US households, Oct 2024–Jul 2025) found widening ChatGPT access cut weekly traditional-search queries 9.4% immediately, rising to 17% after 20 weeks — concentrated in informational categories (academic −32.8%, reference/knowledge −26.5%, developer/technical −15.1%, news −13.4%; transactional/entertainment barely moved). And ChatGPT sends a visitor to an outside site in only 5.2% of sessions vs Google's 31.1%. Takeaway: you can't out-blog this — win by being the cited source and by measuring your AI-answer presence. Run HubSpot's 4-step method with free tools (GSC Generative AI report + free HubSpot AEO Grader + Semrush checker / Bing AI Citation Share).
blocks.p: What changed / the stakes: we've covered AEO (Answer Engine Optimization — getting named inside AI answers) as a measurement discipline; this week brought the hard causal evidence for why it now matters more than another blog post. Researchers at Bocconi University (Shi, Zhu, Gu; arXiv, Jul 8 2026) ran a causal analysis on Comscore's desktop clickstream panel — 45,000+ US households, Oct 2024–Jul 2025 — and found that when ChatGPT access widened, weekly traditional-search queries fell 9.4% immediately and 17% after 20 weeks relative to the pre-expansion baseline. The decline isn't spread evenly: it's concentrated exactly where how-to, explainer and reference content lives — academic research referrals −32.8%, reference/knowledge sites −26.5%, developer/technical −15.1%, news/journalism −13.4% — while transactional and entertainment searches barely changed. The second finding is the gut-punch for publishers: ChatGPT sends a visitor out to an external website in only 5.2% of conversation sessions, versus 31.1% for Google queries — so even when the answer engine uses your material, far fewer clicks flow back. Why it matters for us and our clients: top-of-funnel informational traffic is structurally shrinking, and the machine keeps the click. Publishing more explainer content into that shrinking pool is running to stand still. The durable move is to (a) win the citation inside the answer so your brand is named even when nobody clicks, and (b) change what you measure — from 'do I rank #1 on Google?' to 'am I the cited/mentioned source in the AI answer?' And ranking is not citation: Semrush found the top organic result is used as a citation only ~34% of the time on mobile and ~46% on desktop in Google's AI Overviews, so a #1 ranking no longer guarantees you're in the answer. The run-it-today piece is a repeatable presence audit you can do this afternoon with free tools — HubSpot published a clean 4-step method and a free grader for exactly this.
blocks.doIt: (1) Baseline first-party: open Google Search Console → Performance → Generative AI and export your impressions inside AI Overviews/AI Mode/Discover, date-stamped (impressions-only, beta subset — if absent, verify the property and check back). (2) Define prompts, not keywords: list ~10–20 unbranded, solution-seeking prompts a buyer would actually ask (not your brand name). (3) Grade your presence across engines (free): run the free HubSpot AEO Grader for a three-engine snapshot, and cross-check with Semrush's free AI Search Visibility Checker (no login) and Bing Webmaster Tools → AI Citation Share; run each prompt through ChatGPT, Gemini and Perplexity in logged-out sessions to avoid personalization skew, recording brand mentions, cited pages and which competitors show up. (4) Track the right KPIs: mentions (named, no link), owned citations (your page cited), share-of-voice vs competitors, and AI-referred traffic — a spreadsheet is fine to start. (5) Fix the gaps: for each prompt you're absent from, run the AEO formatting pass on the most relevant money page — intent-matched title, a one-sentence answer up top, a list/table, an original first-party stat, FAQ schema + a last-updated date (card-growth-schema-aeo). (6) Re-measure in 2–4 weeks to prove the formatting pass earned the mention. Card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated with the free HubSpot AEO Grader + the Bocconi stakes).
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed on the study — the Bocconi/Comscore paper is reported by Search Engine Journal and PPC Land, both citing the same arXiv preprint (Shi/Zhu/Gu, Jul 8 2026; 45k+ households; 9.4%→17%; category declines; 5.2% vs 31.1% referral rates). Honest scope: it's a desktop clickstream study over Oct 2024–Jul 2025 (pre-print, not yet peer-reviewed; desktop-only, US-only) — directional on exact magnitudes, robust on the direction. The Semrush 34%/46% citation figure and the free tools (HubSpot AEO Grader, Semrush checker, Bing AI Citation Share, GSC Generative AI report) are separately corroborated. Advances thread-answer-engine-optimization (adds the causal 'why-it-matters-now' leg + the free HubSpot AEO Grader tool). Answers the standing team demand for AI-visibility measurement. Logged a prediction (a 2nd independent dataset confirms AI-chat access cuts informational search ≥10%). Sources: Search Engine Journal, PPC Land, arXiv (Bocconi), HubSpot, Semrush.

sections.h: Email — run it today: your biggest-revenue sends of the year ride on an inbox that grades you over weeks, so do the holiday deliverability prep NOW (mid-July), not in October

blocks.sub: Litmus's summer prep guide (Jul 9 2026) is the CALENDAR leg of deliverability: the mistake is ramping send volume the week you need it — providers read a sudden Q4 spike as a spam signal and junk you at peak. Reputation builds over weeks, so September should be a gradual ramp, not an October cliff. Run it today: (1) auth audit — SPF/DKIM/DMARC across every domain+IP, DMARC ≥ p=quarantine (ideally p=reject), enroll Google Postmaster + Microsoft SNDS/JMRP + Yahoo Sender Hub; (2) clean the list now — validation tool + segment by clicks/purchases/behaviour NOT opens (Apple proxy auto-opens lie; Yahoo purges inactive ~12mo, Gmail ~24mo); (3) re-engage then suppress the disengaged; (4) valid reply-to (Microsoft requires) + a preference center for zero-party data; (5) ramp volume from September in small increments, never a spike.
blocks.p: What it is / the technique: every deliverability control we've carded — DMARC/DMARCbis auth (card-email-dmarcbis-np), the Gmail Postmaster verdict (card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis), Microsoft SNDS (card-email-microsoft-snds), the inbox-placement seed test (card-email-inbox-placement-audit), the re-engagement/sunset flow (card-email-reengagement-sunset) — shares a hidden variable: time. Mailbox providers grade your sender reputation over weeks of behaviour, which means the single most expensive mistake in email is trying to build reputation in the same window you're cashing it in. That's exactly what a typical Q4 does: a program that sends modestly all year suddenly triples its volume for BFCM/holiday — and Gmail/Yahoo/Outlook read a sudden volume spike as a spam signal and throttle or junk you at the highest-revenue moment of the year. Litmus's summer prep guide (Jul 9 2026) is the fix framed as a calendar: mid-July is when you start, so September can be a gradual ramp rather than an October cliff. There's no new product here — the value is the sequencing and the deadline. Why it matters for us: this is a standing, assign-it-now checklist that protects the sends that actually pay for the year, and it's the leg our deliverability library was missing — not what to fix (auth, hygiene, engagement) but when, so the fixes have time to compound into reputation before peak. A note of honesty on the numbers below: the inactive-account purge windows are Litmus's stated figures and the mechanics (spike = risk) are well-established sender guidance — treat the exact month thresholds as directional and confirm against each provider.
blocks.doIt: (1) Auth audit today: verify SPF, DKIM and DMARC across every sending domain and IP; set DMARC to at least p=quarantine, ideally p=reject; enroll in Google Postmaster Tools, Microsoft SNDS/JMRP and Yahoo Sender Hub so you can watch all three consumer inboxes (card-email-microsoft-snds, card-email-postmaster-deliverability-analysis). (2) Clean the list now, not in November: run a list-validation tool to drop invalid/risky addresses, and segment by clicks, purchases and site behaviour — never by opens (Apple Mail Privacy auto-opens inflate open rate); remember Yahoo purges inactive accounts after ~12 months and Gmail after ~24, so old addresses are spam-trap risk. (3) Re-engage, then suppress: run a 2–3 email win-back to 6+ month non-engagers and suppress non-responders before peak (card-email-reengagement-sunset) — a smaller awake list out-earns a big ignored one. (4) Fix the fundamentals: set a valid reply-to address (Microsoft requires it), stand up or promote a preference center to collect zero-party data, and run a quick reply-to engagement campaign for a fast positive-signal win. (5) Ramp on a schedule: begin gradual volume increases in September (not October), testing re-engagement in small increments and avoiding sudden spikes that trip provider red flags. (6) Format for the modern inbox: front-load offers/CTAs, configure Gmail Annotations manually rather than relying on auto-generation, and keep structure clean for accurate AI summarization. Card: card-email-holiday-deliverability-prep.
blocks.note: Corroboration: single strong practitioner source (Litmus, 'Your Holiday Emails Start Now: A Summer Deliverability Prep Guide,' Jul 9 2026) on the sequencing, backed by primary provider tools for each step (Google Postmaster, Microsoft SNDS/JMRP, Yahoo Sender Hub) and our existing corroborated deliverability cards for the underlying mechanics. Honest scope: the exact inactive-purge windows (Yahoo ~12mo / Gmail ~24mo) are Litmus's stated figures — directional, confirm per provider; the core claim (a sudden pre-peak volume spike depresses placement, so ramp early) is well-established sender guidance, not a novel finding. Advances thread-email-deliverability (adds the calendar/warm-up leg that ties the auth + hygiene + engagement cards into a dated Q4 playbook). Logged a prediction (a major ESP ships a built-in volume-ramp / warm-up scheduler that auto-paces increases ahead of peak). Sources: Litmus, Google Postmaster Tools, Microsoft SNDS.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; same-day market prints are directional): Apple sued OpenAI for trade-secret theft, and a macro (oil + rates) shock — not an AI-thesis break — knocked the chip trade Monday

blocks.sub: Two cross-checked reads. (1) Apple sued OpenAI in federal court (N.D. Cal., filed Jul 10) alleging trade-secret theft 'at every level' to build consumer hardware — that OpenAI hardware chief Tang Tan (ex-Apple VP) had job candidates bring 'actual parts' to interviews for 'show and tell,' that an ex-engineer kept an Apple laptop and downloaded confidential docs, and that OpenAI coached departing staff to evade exit security (OpenAI: 'We have no interest in other companies' trade secrets'). The chill traces to OpenAI buying Jony Ive's io Products (~$6.4B) to enter hardware. (2) Monday Jul 13: AI-chip selloff (Nvidia ~−3.5%, Micron ~−4.3%, AMD ~−4.2%, Marvell ~−7.8%, SanDisk ~−12.6%; Nasdaq ~−1.55%) driven by a US–Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz oil spike + inflation-rebound / Fed-rate-hike worries — macro, not a capex-confidence break.
blocks.p: What moved (cross-checked; treat same-day market figures as directional). (1) Apple v. OpenAI. Apple filed suit in the Northern District of California on Jul 10, alleging that OpenAI 'at every level, from members of its Technical Staff to its Chief Hardware Officer' has been stealing Apple's trade secrets to develop its own consumer hardware. The specifics are vivid: OpenAI's hardware chief Tang Tan, a former Apple VP, allegedly directed Apple employees interviewing at OpenAI to bring 'actual parts' from Apple for 'show and tell'; a former Apple senior engineer, Chang Liu, allegedly failed to return an Apple laptop and used it to download confidential technical documents on unannounced products; and Apple claims OpenAI coached departing staff to evade Apple's exit-security processes. OpenAI's response: 'We have no interest in other companies' trade secrets. We remain focused on building innovative technology that empowers people everywhere.' The backdrop is the striking reversal from the two firms' 2024 ChatGPT-in-Siri partnership, which chilled after OpenAI bought former Apple designer Jony Ive's io Products for ~$6.4B to move into hardware. Read for us: this is an escalation of the Apple-as-AI-company thread and the broader AI-hardware land grab — and a reminder that as talent and IP move between AI labs and device makers, provenance and IP become procurement questions on the work we ship and the vendors we recommend. It's a climate signal, not a card. (2) Markets (directional). Monday Jul 13 the semis led a broad risk-off session — Nvidia ~−3.5%, Micron ~−4.3%, AMD ~−4.2%, Marvell ~−7.8%, SanDisk ~−12.6%; Nasdaq ~−1.55%, S&P ~−0.79% (TradingKey/Yahoo Finance/CNBC; intraday-vs-close vary, treat % as directional) — but the driver was macro, not AI: a weekend US–Iran escalation over the Strait of Hormuz spiked oil, stacking on inflation-rebound and Fed-resuming-rate-hike worries. That distinction matters for the open Jun-8 'dip not regime change' call [news, made Jun 8, due ~Jul 20]: a macro/oil-driven chip pullback pressures the basket (SoftBank/Samsung/SK Hynix) but is not a capex-confidence break, so we hold the call open and judge it at the window on closes, not on Monday's intraday tape. On the clock next week: the Jun-19 GLM-5.2 OpenRouter-top-3 call [due ~Jul 19] and the Jun-9 'AAPL reverses the WWDC sell-the-news' call [due ~Jul 21] — neither resolvable today. No news card (the landscape is intake; the durable craft payoff is route-your-models / build-for-portability, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). Sources: CNBC, TechCrunch, Bloomberg, Fortune, 9to5Mac (Apple v. OpenAI); TradingKey, Yahoo Finance, CNBC (markets Jul 13).

sources: Cloudflare Blog — Introducing Precursor: detecting agentic behavior with continuous client-side signals (Jul 13 2026) · SiliconANGLE — Cloudflare launches Precursor to catch bots by watching entire sessions · Help Net Security — Cloudflare Precursor uses continuous behavioral analysis to stop advanced bots · Cloudflare — Turnstile (free CAPTCHA replacement, any plan) · Search Engine Journal — ChatGPT Access Tied To 9% Drop In Traditional Search (Bocconi study) · PPC Land — Wider ChatGPT access cuts traditional search 9.4% (Comscore, 45k+ households; 5.2% vs 31.1% referrals) · HubSpot — How to track your brand's presence in AI search (4-step method + free AEO Grader) · HubSpot — Free AEO Grader (ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity snapshot) · Semrush — Free AI Search Visibility Checker · Litmus — Your Holiday Emails Start Now: A Summer Deliverability Prep Guide (Jul 9 2026) · Google Postmaster Tools · Microsoft SNDS (Outlook/Hotmail sender IP reputation + JMRP) · CNBC — Apple sues OpenAI alleging trade-secret theft 'at every level' · TechCrunch — The wildest allegations in Apple's trade-secrets lawsuit against OpenAI · 9to5Mac — Apple sues OpenAI, accuses ex-employees of stealing trade secrets · TradingKey — AI chip stocks plunge, Brent crude surges (Jul 13 2026) · Yahoo Finance — Stocks fall as chip stocks tumble, oil surges on US–Iran tensions (Jul 13)
